coredata.dc:title: Tailoring the surface oxygen engineering of a carbon-quantum-dot-sensitized ZnO@H-ZnO1-x multijunction toward efficient charge dynamics and photoactivity enhancement
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Yu-Chang
Peng, Chun-Kuo
Lim, Suh-Ciuan
Chen, Chi-Liang
Nguyễn, Trọng-Nghĩa
Wang, Tsai-Te
Lin, Ming-Chang
Hsu, Yung-Jung
Chen, San-Yuan
Lin, Yan-Gu
coredata.dc:description:
A well-steered coordination environment on photoelectrode can offer fruitful active sites and efficaciously alter atomic and electronic configuration to boost the performance in water oxidation. To enhance the performance of the photocatalytic oxygen-evolution reaction, enriched oxygenous-type carbon quantum dots (CQD) were combined with oxygen-deficient ZnO@H-ZnO1-
x homojunctions for the first time. Specifically, hydrogenation created an unstoichiometric H-ZnO1-
x surface through oxygen vacancies, which were responsible for the formation of coordinatively unsaturated Zn centers to drive the photoelectrochemical reaction by precise regulation of the charge density, valence-band edge, atomic geometric structure and electronic structure. The enriched photohole reservoir in carboxyl-conjugated CQD was a great benefit for super-rapid charge tunnels, thereby governing the rate of internal and interfacial charge separation and transport in the multijunction. Accordingly, the CQD/H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO photoanodes showed a photoactivity enhanced five-fold relative to bare ZnO NR and maintained that high performance for 24 h of operation. Comprehensive analyses, involving electrochemical measurements, time-resolved photoluminescence, X-ray absorption spectra in situ and calculations with density-functional theory were undertaken to elucidate the underlying mechanism of enhancement and the route of directional charge transport as well as the interfacial charge dynamics over the CQD/H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO photoelectrode. These findings provide a new track towards photoelectrodes that could yield not only strikingly enhanced photocatalysis but also satisfied stability for realistic application.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
An unstoichiometric H-ZnO1-
x surface with oxygen vacancies and the enriched photohole reservoir in carboxyl-conjugated CQD were great benefit to govern the rate of internal and interfacial charge separation and transport in CQD/H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO multijunction. In this system, an extraordinary photocurrent density of 4.5 mA∙cm–2 was achieved and maintained cycling stability with only a 4.9 % reduction after operation of 24 h.

A well-steered coordination environment on photoelectrode can offer fruitful active sites and efficaciously alter atomic and electronic configuration to boost the performance in water oxidation. To enhance the performance of the photocatalytic oxygen-evolution reaction, enriched oxygenous-type carbon quantum dots (CQD) were combined with oxygen-deficient ZnO@H-ZnO1-
x homojunctions for the first time. Specifically, hydrogenation created an unstoichiometric H-ZnO1-
x surface through oxygen vacancies, which were responsible for the formation of coordinatively unsaturated Zn centers to drive the photoelectrochemical reaction by precise regulation of the charge density, valence-band edge, atomic geometric structure and electronic structure. The enriched photohole reservoir in carboxyl-conjugated CQD was a great benefit for super-rapid charge tunnels, thereby governing the rate of internal and interfacial charge separation and transport in the multijunction. Accordingly, the CQD/H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO photoanodes showed a photoactivity enhanced five-fold relative to bare ZnO NR and maintained that high performance for 24 h of operation. Comprehensive analyses, involving electrochemical measurements, time-resolved photoluminescence, X-ray absorption spectra in situ and calculations with density-functional theory were undertaken to elucidate the underlying mechanism of enhancement and the route of directional charge transport as well as the interfacial charge dynamics over the CQD/H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO photoelectrode. These findings provide a new track towards photoelectrodes that could yield not only strikingly enhanced photocatalysis but also satisfied stability for realistic application.

ce:section.ce:para: Defect engineering is the pivotal factor to adjust multifunctional properties of oxides, such as atomic or electronic structure, charge transport, optical absorption and catalytic performance. In theory, perfectly stoichiometric metal oxides are generally inactive in catalytic applications [1–3]. In the past, the construction of oxygen vacancies (VO) was regarded as an efficacious route to accelerate the kinetics of the water-splitting reaction by activating adjacent atoms to modify the density of states (DOS) near the Fermi level [4,5]. The multiple effects further yield a fast rate of electron transfer and an instability of the metal–oxygen bonds, leading to a more rapid intermediate exchange behavior and a superior catalysis. In addition, a treatment with molecular hydrogen, i.e. hydrogenation, is a pragmatic way for fabricating efficient visible–wavelength–active oxides in a photocatalytic reaction [6–8]. Presumably the impacts of hydrogenation on the photocatalysis originate from microstructural disorder and VO, causing modifications on the electronic and atomic characteristics of the oxides. In general, the photocatalytic performance of an oxide depends greatly on the optical and electrical properties that are determined mostly by the atomic or electronic structure and defect species. Nevertheless, the introduction of VO without inevitably destroying the intrinsic crystal structure of oxide photoelectrodes remains unconquered and the microstructure–performance relation is still under debate. Visual and specific discussions of VO that contribute to alterations in the surface atomic geometric and electronic structures, and thus a determination of the disorder effect in photoelectrodes such as ZnO, are still not comprehensively evident toward the solar water-oxidation reaction.

The modification of semiconductor photoelectrodes with zero-dimensional (0D) materials, i.e. carbon quantum dots (CQD), has been another research orientation to boost the photocatalysis via expanding the electron–hole separation and multiplying the visible-light absorption [9,10]. More specifically, CQD as emerging optoelectronic materials seem to be an exceptional candidate for such a photosensitizer as they are green, nontoxic and terrestrially abundant [11,12]. Furthermore, CQD with fruitful functional surface groups have been widely studied to boost the electrocatalytic performance in an electrochemical reaction [13,14], but a scientific issue of probing the charge-transition route in controllable oxygenous functionalized CQD has received sparse attention. In particular, a detailed understanding of the nature of photocarrier transport channels in a defective CQD–ZnO photoelectrode has not been reported to our knowledge.

To tackle the abovementioned challenges, we demonstrate that the precise control of an oxygen-deficient and oxygen-enriched coordination environment in hybrid photoelectrodes can effectively trigger photocatalytic water oxidation. A facile hydrogenation modulates the surface of a ZnO nanowire (NW) array with a thin oxygen-deficient layer to form ZnO@H-ZnO1–
x core–shell homojunctions. We designed a novel integrated nanoarchitecture on depositing ZnO@H-ZnO1–
x uniformly with an ultrathin overlayer of isolated oxygen-enriched CQD to prepare advanced photoelectrochemical (PEC) devices. With the aid of synchrotron-based X-ray techniques and theoretical computations, we demonstrate the synergistic effects of oxygen enrichment in CQD and VO in ZnO@H-ZnO1–
x to verify the dominant influence on the photocatalytic water oxidation reaction.

The geometries of ZnO (bulk and surface) in the wurtzite structure as well as the adsorption, migration and dissociation of hydrogen on the ZnO surface are investigated [15,16]. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional [17] is used in this work. All related species have been computed using periodic boundary conditions with cutoff of kinetic energy at 500 eV. The Brillouin zone is evaluated using the Monkhorst-Pack k-points [18] with 2 × 4 × 1 for the surface and 3 × 3 × 2 k-points for the bulk. For the ZnO surface, the spacing 15 Å, which is large enough that the interaction between two neighboring slabs can be ignored, was used in the calculations. The transition states are calculated using the climbing-image nudged-elastic-band (CINEB) method, which is available in the VASP code [19]. In addition, the band structure and the DOS of the species were predicted using the PBE0 hybrid functional (a mixture of PBE and HF functionals) because it is well-known that the PBE underestimates the values. Our computed lattice parameters for the ZnO (bulk) as well as the band gap for ZnO (bulk and surface) agree satisfactorily with the experimental data (see below).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnO NW array photoanode
ce:section.ce:para: ZnO NW arrays were prepared via a hydrothermal method. Typically, zinc acetate (14 mM) was spin-coated onto a conductive substrate (fluorine-doped tin oxide) and kept for 10 s. The coated substrate was then annealed at 350 °C for 5 min in air to form a seed layer. Next, the seeded substrate was placed vertically in a Teflon vessel containing a solution of zinc nitrate hexahydrate (40 mM), hexamethylenetetramine (25 mM), poly(ethyleneimine) solution (90 mM), and ammonia solution (250 mM). Finally, the Teflon vessel was put in an autoclave and heated at 65 °C for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of H-ZnO1–x/ZnO core-shell NW array photoanode
ce:section.ce:para:
The benchmark electrode was set up based on a ZnO photoanode on applying gaseous H2 (99.999 %) as the source of hydrogen. First, the ZnO NW photoanode was placed in a quartz tubular furnace and kept under vacuum for 1 h. The furnace was then packed with hydrogen near room temperature. After annealing at 300 °C for 24 h under static conditions, the H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO core-shell NW array was formed.

CQD was synthesized in a one-step approach. Briefly, a solution containing NaOH (1 M) and glucose (1 M) was handled to a 150-W (40-kHz) ultrasonic treatment for 2 h at 25 °C. The pH of glucose/NaOH crude solution was then adjusted to 7. After that, the crude solution underwent dialysis with a semi-permeable membrane (MWCO 1000); a light brown solution was finally obtained, indicating the formation of CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQD/H-ZnO1–x/ZnO core-shell NW array photoanode
ce:section.ce:para: A CQD solution was spin-coated onto the core-shell NW photoelectrode as prepared and annealed at 150 °C for 5 min. The thickness of the CQD layers was controlled on varying the rotation cycles during the spin coating.

Morphology, microstructure and composition of the CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO photoelectrodes were inspected via FE-SEM (JEOL, JSM-6700 F) and HR-TEM (JEOL, JEM-ARM200FTH). UV–vis absorption spectra (Hitachi U-3900 H) were conducted. Typical photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Hitachi F-4500) were recorded with a xenon lamp (150 W) (λex = 325 nm). Time-resolved PL (TRPL) spectra were carried out (λex = 320 nm, PicoQuant, PLS 320). The PL emission of ZnO (λem = 375 nm) was fitted to a bi-exponential decay; the average luminescence lifetime (<τ>) was calculated using the Eq. (1) [20],
(1)

The PEC properties of the photoanode were evaluated with an electrochemical analyzer (CHI 6273E) in Na2SO4 solution (0.5 M) in a three-electrode setup. All experiments were conducted under illumination with a 150 W Xe lamp (PowerArc™, OBB) incorporated with an AM-1.5 G filter. Mott-Schottky (M–S) measurements were obtained at frequency 10 kHz using a 10-mV AC signal. The donor concentration (N D) of these samples were computed from the slopes of the M–S analysis [21]. The wavelength-dependent photocatalytic activity was performed with applied bias of 1 VAg/AgCl under monochromatic irradiation. The amount of H2 generated in the PEC cell was analyzed by a gas chromatography spectrometer (Bruker SCION, 463-MS).

The XRD patterns were measured at beamline TLS 17B in NSRRC, Taiwan. Hard X-ray absorption spectra (XAS) including X-ray-absorption near-edge structure (XANES) and extended X-ray-absorption fine structure (EXAFS) were measured in a total fluorescence-yield (TFY) mode at beamline TPS 44A. EXAFS analysis involved a Fourier transform of the k 3-weighted EXAFS oscillations. The soft XAS (sXAS) were recorded at beamline TLS 20A. All samples were subjected to an UHV chamber (10−9 torr); the resulting signals were conducted via the surface sensitivity of the total electron-yield (TEY) detection with resolving power E/ΔE ∼ 8 × 103. XAS data of each sample were recorded in situ under illuminated and dark conditions with a triple-A solar-simulator lamp (350 − 1100 nm, HAL-320, Asahi Spectra). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) and ultraviolet photoelectron spectra (UPS) were recorded for samples in a UHV chamber (10−9 torr) and measured with an electron-energy analyzer (SPECS PHOIBOS 150) at NSRRC station 24A of TLS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical characterization of photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 1 a reveals a SEM image of vertically aligned CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO photoelectrodes. These hybrid nanoarchitectures have diameter about 140 nm and height 8 μm, as determined directly from the top-view and cross-sectional SEM micrograph. The SIMS depth profiles for H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO and bare ZnO are compared in Fig. S1, displaying a significant increase in hydrogen content for H-ZnO1-
x nanoshell, induced by the hydrogenation process. This highlights that the outer H layer surrounding a core has thickness about 7 nm. A typical TEM image of CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO hybrid nanocomposites shows close attachment of CQDs on the H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO surface as depicted in Fig. 1b. The crystalline interplanar spacing, 0.52 nm, is indexed to plane (0001) of wurtzite ZnO. The uniform lattice at the interface manifests that the ZnO core and H-ZnO1–
x shell are intimately combined. In addition, the HR-TEM image of the edge for a hybrid architecture (Fig. 1c) provides compelling evidence that the ultrafine CQD, mean size ∼1.8−2.2 nm, are well immobilized on the ZnO@H-ZnO1–
x surface. The well-resolved lattice fringes, 0.31 nm, correspond to plane (002) of the CQD, indicating the graphite structure. Detailed elemental distributions of Zn, O and C are revealed in Fig. S2b, confirming that the CQD are decorated uniformly on the H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO surface. XRD patterns of pristine ZnO before and after hydrogenation are compared in Fig. S3a, showing that both have the wurtzite structure and hence that there was no significant change of crystal structures upon mild hydrogenation. Hydrogenation is expected to produce a H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO homojunction by partially replacing oxygen with hydrogen, causing the appearance of an unstoichiometric H-ZnO1–
x surface. Moreover, the introduction of VO with mild hydrogenation might be a type of self-doping without heteroatom impurities, which is more favorable for the preservation of the intrinsic crystal structure of ZnO nanostructures and leads to improved photocatalytic activity with visible light.

To verify the optical absorption of photoelectrodes, UV–vis absorption spectra and corresponding Tauc plots are displayed in Fig. 1d. Relative to pristine ZnO, a red shift of the band gap in H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO homojunction is observed. This effect might be caused mainly by the light absorption of the new intermediate energy levels formed by VO, shaping tail states within the band gap. CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO appears to exhibit a significantly enhanced absorption of visible light. The coated CQD are responsible for the extended visible light absorption as they can harvest visible photons from sunlight over a wide range, as illustrated in Fig. S3b. In general, the smaller CQD emit shorter wavelengths, thus yielding a blue color under illumination with UV light [11]. The absorption of pure CQD from the UV–vis spectrum cuts off at wavelength 447.8 nm (band gap ∼ 2.77 eV). From the PL result, the diameter of pure CQD is estimated to be 1.5–2.0 nm [11], matching well our HR-TEM observation (Fig. S7).

To clarify the intrinsic electronic conﬁgurations of photoelectrodes as prepared in the electrolyte, we measured the M–S effect to verify both N D and flat band potential (V FB) at the contacts between solid and liquid phases. As presented in Fig. 2 a, V FB of H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO is determined to be 0.16 VAg/AgCl, without significant alteration relative to pristine ZnO (0.19 VAg/AgCl). Notably, the calculated N D of the pure ZnO and H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO are 5.18 × 1017 cm–3 and 2.0 × 1018 cm–3, respectively. The enhanced N D is attributed to the VO, thus likely improving the charge transport of the homojunction. To understand the local atomic structure around the Zn atoms of the photoelectrodes, we undertook model analyses with EXAFS through measurement of XAS at a small grazing angle. The Zn K-edge EXAFS spectra were subjected to Fourier-transform (FT) analysis. As displayed in Fig. 2b, two main features (at ∼1.6 Å and 2.9 Å, without phase correction) in the Zn K-edge EXAFS spectra of ZnO and H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO correspond to the single scattering paths of the nearest oxygen (Zn-O) and the secondary zinc metal atoms (Zn-Zn) that surround the absorbing Zn atoms. The structural parameters obtained from curve fitting to these spectra were listed in Table S1, which clearly suggests that oxygen in pristine ZnO has a coordination number of 3.6 while that in H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO has a coordination number of 3.2. Relative to pristine ZnO, the oxygen in the ZnO@H-ZnO1–
x homojunction is a low-coordinated species, revealing that most coordination sites of unsaturated Zn centers are occupied by VO in this photoelectrode.

In comparison with hard XAS, the shallow penetration of sXAS is more sensitive to the surface, so directly probing the transition-metal 3d electronic properties in the vicinity of the Fermi level with dipole selection rules [21]. The O 2p unoccupied states in the conduction band of VO-containing ZnO and its hybridization with Zn orbitals can be clarified clearly with O K-edge spectra, as shown in Fig. 2c. A substantially increased absorption intensity over a ZnO@H-ZnO1–
x homojunction indicates a presence of highly dense local VO around the Zn atoms on the H-ZnO1–
x nanoshell surface. Additionally, as the Zn 3d states (i.e., 3d 10) of ZnO are fully occupied, the prominent features of Zn L 3-edge spectra are dominated by the electron excitations from Zn 2p states to unoccupied 4s/d-derived states (at ∼1027 to 1035 eV), as shown in Fig. 2d. Compared to pristine ZnO, a significantly attenuated absorption intensity in the Zn L 3-edge spectra of ZnO@H-ZnO1–
x homojunction corresponds to a strong charge localization induced by hydrogenation, in which hydrogen dopants pushed partial electrons to the H-ZnO1–
x nanoshell, likely leading to more rapid charge transfer for the oxygen evolution. An electronic push effect was confirmed with the XPS results, which were obtained to understand the surface electronic configurations of these hybrid photoelectrodes. In the corresponding O 1s spectra (Fig. 2e), the peak at 532.1 eV is ascribed to VO; the peak at 530.2 eV is due to Zn-O-Zn. A trend is found in terms of VO content, in which the homojunction shows a high level (22.7 %) relative to pristine ZnO (2.8 %), indicating an 8-fold increase due to hydrogenation. Furthermore, as displayed in Fig. 2f, the high-resolution Zn 2p spectra were decomposed into two main features of Zn2+. Relative to pristine ZnO, the features of H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO show a negative shift of binding energy, indicating that they have different chemical environments. Subtraction of the normalized Zn 2p spectra of H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO from those of pristine ZnO shows two extra peaks centered at ca. 1020.6 and 1043.6 eV (lower Fig. 2f). These two peaks are consistent with the representative Zn 2p 1/2 and Zn 2p 3/2 peaks of Zn°, verifying the presence of Zn°-like defects in the homojunction, thus facilitating charge redistribution. Therefore, the Zn°-like defects can be attributed to the VO and hydrogen spillover phenomenon during hydrogenation process. This conclusion is in accordance with the sXAS analyses (Fig. 2c and d) and the SIMS results (Fig. S1).

Fig. 3 displays schematically the changes in the electronic structure during hydrogenation. When core electrons are excited by X-ray with proper energy, the core electrons from both the O 1s and Zn 2p states are principally excited to unoccupied O 2p and Zn 4s/d states via O K-edge and Zn L-edge spectra, respectively. The sXAS evidence clearly reveals that a charge redistribution occurred at the H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO homojunction, accompanied by VO resulting from the hydrogenation. The mechanism and energetics of the hydrogenation on a ZnO surface were further investigated in detail employing density-functional theory (DFT) calculations. Computationally, the lattice parameters for bulk ZnO were adopted with a = 6.57 and c = 10.62 Å, which are in satisfactory accordance with experimental values a = 6.50 and c = 10.41 Å [22], as presented in Fig. S4. The band gap obtained with the PBE0 method [23] for the ZnO crystal is 3.1 eV, closely matching experimental value 3.3 eV. The calculated potential-energy surfaces for the dissociative adsorption of H2 on the ZnO surface and the transit of a H-atom to sub-surface are shown in Fig. 4 a and Fig. S5a; the related structure-optimized products are shown in Fig. 4b and Fig. S5b. Initially, the H2 molecule could be physiosorbed on the surface (denoted as H2…ZnO) with binding energy −0.2 kcal/mol; its HH and H…O2c bond lengths are 0.754 and 2.847 Å, respectively. From that corresponding stable adsorbate, the fracturing of the HH bond followed by H-atom transit to an adjacent bridging O2c atom might take place with a barrier 51.6 kcal/mol at TS1, forming 2H–O2c with endothermicity 9.9 kcal/mol from the H2…ZnO adsorbate. A subsequent H-atom transit from the OH group to an adjacent bridging O3c atom passing through an activation barrier 54.8 kcal/mol at TS2 creates unpaired or dangling HO2c, HO3c up-ZnO with exothermicity 8.6 kcal/mol from 2H–O2c; here HO3c up signifies an H atom adsorbed on an O3c site with the H–O bond pointing upward. Its corresponding geometric structures are given in Fig. 3. As a result, unsaturated oxygen atoms on the surface of ZnO can be attributed to VO induced by the hydrogenation. In contrast, the maintenance of the HH bond followed by migration of a H2 molecule to sub-surface layers would occur with barrier 70.6 kcal/mol at TS3, forming H2@1st (H2 inside big cage of ZnO) with endothermicity 30.9 kcal/mol from the H2…ZnO adsorbate. This path is hence unlikely to occur during the hydrogenation.

To explore the effect of hydrogenation on the energy-band structures of ZnO, we calculated the DOS using a DFT method, as presented in Fig. 4c. Computationally, the energy of the valence band maximum (VBM) of the bulk phase is taken to be zero. Calculated band gaps for bulk ZnO and H2…ZnO crystals were set to 3.1 and 2.99 eV, respectively. At the initial stage of the hydrogenation, it is evident that H2 dissociation to a 2H–O2c configuration could lower the band gap to 1.68 eV, relative to the undoped counterpart. With respect to unpaired or dangling HO2c, in the HO3c up-ZnO configuration, large modifications of the distributions of electron density within the VB are observed, as plotted in Fig. 3. Notably, the formation of an additional sub-band state, induced by VO, would essentially alter the charge density and broaden the VB to create a decreased band gap. The DOS of the VB for VO-containing ZnO was also experimentally probed with VB-XPS, as shown in Fig. S6. The VBM edge levels of H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO and bare ZnO were verified to be 2.47 eV and 2.95 eV, respectively, reflecting that an upshift of the VBM accompanied by the occurrence of the VB tail states can be attributed to the induced VO. Remarkably, VO can introduce a sub-band of electronic states that overlaps with the VB, which leads to a valence-band tail, and consequently allows the absorption of visible light by the unstoichiometric H-ZnO1–
x surface for the photocatalytic reaction. This finding is consistent with our UV–vis absorption results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC performance of CQD/H-ZnO1–x/ZnO hybrid photoanode
ce:section.ce:para:
To clarify the combined effects of the homojunction configuration and the CQD modification on the photocatalytic performance, we undertook the PEC characterization and wavelength-dependent photocatalytic activity measurements systematically in a Na2SO4 solution (0.5 M) with no sacrificial agent. Fig. 5 a depicts a set of linear-sweep voltammograms (LSV) recorded on ZnO-based photoanodes in darkness and under illumination at 100 mW·cm−2. Significantly, the H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO hybrids show a 4.4-fold improved performance relative to a bare ZnO NR system, mainly because dopant hydrogen atoms led to a charge redistribution on transferring electrons to the H-ZnO1–
x nanoshell, resulting in an improved photocatalytic performance. Maximum photocurrent density 4.5 mA·cm−2 was achieved for CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO hybrid photoanodes, representing a five-fold enhancement relative to bare ZnO NR. Additionally, the PEC activity of CQD/ZnO photoelectrode without hydrogenation treatment is also performed as shown in Fig. S10. Apparently, without hydrogenation treatment, the PEC activity of CQD/ZnO photoelectrode would be worsened greatly with photocurrent of 1.5 mA·cm–2. The results show that the hydrogenation treatment in photoelectrode will be a great benefit to PEC activity. The long-term durability of the CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO hybrid photoanode was also evaluated under LSV measurements. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, the CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO hybrid photoanode exhibits an excellent cycling stability with only 4.9 % reduction of photocurrent density after operation for 24 h. The SEM, XRD, and corresponding elemental mapping analyses of CQD/H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO photoelectrodes after 24 h cyclic test are carried out as shown in Fig. S8. Compared with before cyclic test (Fig. S2), the CQD/H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO basically retains the integrated morphology after the 24 h cyclic test. In addition, the XRD patterns for CQD/H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO after the long-term cyclic test are shown to be similar with those before the reaction, except that part of the decreased crystallinity was obtained during water oxidation procedure. Significantly, all these results further suggest the outstanding stability of CQD/H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO. Moreover, the gaseous hydrogen evolution and the Faradaic efficiency were performed as shown in Fig. 5c. Notably, calculated Faradaic efficiency of CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO photoanode was about 97.1 %. This demonstration corroborates that the gas generation reaction of CQD/H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO photoelectrode thoroughly generated from the photocurrent production without other objectionable side reactions. On the other hand, in order to understand the stability of VO on ZnO formed by this process, O 1s core level XPS of H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO after 24 h cyclic test is measured as shown in Fig. S9b. Obviously, the concentration of VO on H-ZnO1-
x /ZnO basically can be maintained after the cyclic test. Meanwhile, the new peak at 533 eV is observed and attributed to the chemisorbed OH group (VC) during water oxidation procedure. Furthermore, the H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO photoanode after the long-term cyclic test exhibits a clear decay of PEC performance (Fig. S9a), which may be due to the photocorrosion behavior accompanied by the slight increase of VO. Therefore, the introduction of CQD on the H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO homojunction is theoretically expected to benefit enhancing the optical absorption and diminishing the speed of electron–hole recombination, so resulting in a desirable PEC activity and durability simultaneously. Table S2 is the comparison of the photocatalytic performance in many reported photoelectrodes. The accomplishments of CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO multijunction are comparable to those achieved in top-performing ZnO-based photoelectrodes according to recent reports [24–54].

To quantify the PEC performance, we measured the wavelength-dependent photocatalytic activity to study the photoresponse of the photoanodes, as shown in Fig. 5d. It is important to note that the CQD-photosensitized H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO homojunction displays much greater photocurrent density values in the visible region of interest than bare ZnO. The increased light utilization and conversion photocurrent in the visible region is attributable to the multiple effects of CQD decoration and hydrogenation on ZnO photoanode. Theoretical calculations, with the support of UPS results, indicate that hydrogenation of the H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO homojunction could induce a sub-band of electronic states associated with VO and accompanied with valence-band tailing, thus likely weakening the chemisorbed oxygen intermediates and improving the photoconversion performance. Moreover, CQD are considered to supply many favorable tunnels for directional charge transfer under simulated sunlight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interfacial charge transfer behavior in a CQD/H-ZnO1–x/ZnO configuration
ce:section.ce:para:
Fundamentally, the efficiency of PEC water splitting greatly relies on light-absorption and light-induced charge separation, which is followed by charge transfer or transport at the photoanode. To acquire much insightful information on the roles of the homojunction and the CQD in mediating the charge-transfer dynamics within ZnO, we recorded TRPL spectra, displayed in Fig. 5e. The interfacial charge dynamics within the hybrid photoelectrodes were quantitatively deduced on comparing the decay profiles between relevant samples. Relative to bare ZnO, both homojunction and CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO photoelectrodes showed much more rapid PL decay with pronounced charge-separation characteristics. The recorded recession kinetics were fitted and analyzed with a biexponential function, in which two time parameters (radiative recombination, τ1 and nonradiative deactivation, τ2) and an intensity-weighted average emission lifetime (<τ>) were evaluated. As summarized in Table 1 , the average lifetimes (<τ>) were computed to be 9.19, 8.82 and 8.19 ns for pristine ZnO, H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO and CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO, respectively. Both H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO and CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO exhibit much shorter lifetimes than pristine ZnO, signifying an improved charge separation at the H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO and CQD/H-ZnO1–
x junctions due to the synergistic effects of the CQD modification and the H-ZnO1–
x layer in the ZnO photoanode. The rate coefficient of the interfacial charge transfer at the H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO and CQD/H-ZnO1–
x interfaces was quantitatively determined with the expression,

The estimated values of k CT are 4.58 × 106 s−1 and 8.72 × 106 s−1 for H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO and CQD/H-ZnO1–
x , respectively. The value of k CT for CQD/H-ZnO1–
x is much greater than that for H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO, implying that CQD-mediated interfacial charge dynamics strongly manifests the superior photocatalytic activities of the multijunction photoelectrodes. Notably, CQD can not only act as a light absorber but also function as a super-rapid charge tunnel for the photocatalytic reaction.

The impact of the alteration of interfacial interaction in the complex photoelectrode on the PEC performance is confirmed through a sXAS technique in situ, which is designed to disclose an underlying mechanism for the enhancement and the path of directional charge transport during the photocatalytic reaction, as shown in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6a illustrates a simplified model describing how the synchrotron X-rays track the real-time evolution of the unoccupied density of states (UDOS) under 1-sun illumination for the photoelectrodes as prepared. Relative to pristine ZnO, solar illumination obviously increases the UDOS of Zn 4s/d-derived states for the homojunction with a much positive ΔA value (A 1) of the Zn L 3-edge XANES, as shown in Fig. 6b. A photo-induced charge separation is unambiguously facilitated by the unstoichiometric H-ZnO1–
x layer. Once the ultrafine CQD decorates the surface of the homojunction, solar illumination promotes the electron density to enhance the Zn 4s/d-derived states that are observed from a negative ΔA value (A 3) of the Zn L 3-edge XANES, as displayed in Fig. 6c. In contrast, the UDOS of the antibonding π*-state character increases with a positive ΔA value (A 4) of the C K-edge XANES under AM 1.5 G illumination (Fig. 6d). The feature centered at ∼289 eV is attributable to transitions of C 1s core-level electrons to unoccupied antibonding π* states of CO bonds derived from carbonyl functionalities. Enriched oxygenous-type carbon in CQD, also acting as photosensitizers, can sensibly assist the photo-hole transport from H-ZnO1–
x to the CQD through the CO bond and inhibit the accumulation of photo-electrons in the CQD under simulated sunlight. Accordingly, the presence of the massive carboxyl groups on the CQD is deduced to provide more active sites to improve the PEC performance significantly through an ability to drive super-rapid charge transfer. On the basis of the TRPL and sXAS evidence in situ, we speculate that, once photoelectrons are quickly dispelled and/or transited away from the CQD to ZnO@H-ZnO1–
x , the photohole depository in the antibonding π* state might be nurtured from unique transport channels (i.e. carboxyl groups); it can rationally be believed to endow hybrid photoelectrodes with an efficient photocatalytic activity. The synergistic effects of oxygen-enriched CQD and the oxygen-deficient H-ZnO1–
x surface further highlight the importance of manipulating the surface charge density, VB edge, atomic geometric structure and local electronic structure to improve the route of directional charge transport and interfacial charge dynamics.

It is essential to propose a plausible charge-transfer mechanism for the solar water-splitting reaction over the CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO photoelectrode, as illustrated in Fig. 7 . To pinpoint the electronic band structure, we recorded UPS of the constituent multijunctions of the photoelectrodes (Fig. S6). Combining the UPS and UV–vis absorption analyses, we delineated clearly the band structures of the CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO multijunctions, reflecting that both the conduction-band (CB) and VB positions of H-ZnO1–
x and CQD are above those of pristine ZnO, which implies a type-II staggered band alignment, as we observed in the sXAS results in situ. Upon simulated solar irradiation, the photoexcited holes would preferentially move to the H-ZnO1-
x and then transfer energetically to CQD to drive the water oxidation. The upper CB levels of CQD and H-ZnO1–
x drift the photogenerated electrons in opposite directions to the inner ZnO synchronously. Eventually the charge transport is blocked by the inner ZnO, so allowing the transport of the separated electrons to the back electrode, and then to conduct the hydrogen evolution. On the basis of the experimental results and theoretical computations, the impact of the oxygen coordination environments on the electronic and atomic geometric characteristics and the photocatalytic activity of the complex photoelectrode for solar water oxidation become understood systematically in this work. Specifically, rich Vo are created nearby Zn, which induce the emergence of unsaturated Zn, make part of the electron redistribute on the neighboring Zn atom and facilitate the VB tailing, thus enhancing the photocatalytic activity. More importantly, it is also demonstrated that, apart from enabling the superior photocatalytic activities via enhanced optical absorption and lessening the kinetics of electron–hole recombination, the CQD can stabilize the formed active ZnO@H-ZnO1–
x homojunction during the water oxidation, hence rendering such nanohybrids applicable for a high-performance photoanode.

The key factors affecting the water-oxidation activity are shown to be the exact management of both the oxygenous functionalization and the oxygen defects on the CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO photoelectrode, thus significantly enabling high-performance photoanodes with excellent stability. Hydrogenation can induce a surface reconstruction of ZnO to produce an unstoichiometric H-ZnO1–
x surface, which possesses a strong charge localization and unsaturated Zn centers occupied by Vo, indicated by a combination of XANES, EXAFS and XPS. Theoretical calculations, with the support of the UPS results, reveal that Vo within the H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO homojunction can induce a sub-band of electronic states accompanied by VB tailing. In particular, sXAS evidence in situ also highlights that the reservoir of photoholes via unique transport channels in carboxyl-conjugated CQD is tailored to direct the internal and interfacial charge separation or rate of transport in the multijunction. Consequently, the CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO photoanode has achieved a remarkable photocurrent density, 4.5 mA∙cm−2, without a hole scavenger, and has maintained satisfactory photocatalytic activity during 24 h of operation. We believe that tailoring the coordination environment of the surface oxygen of CQD/H-ZnO1–
x /ZnO nanohybrids is unprecedented for photocatalytic reactions and can offer unique chances to realize exotic properties not attainable in the conventional photoelectrode.
